feedback.text: Dear members of the European Commission, within the framework of the Digital Fairness Act, I would like to bring to your attention the unfair practice of publishers disabling games and programs once purchased by customers. The procedure involves the fact that a published (single-player) game or program requires a periodic or permanent connection to the publisher's central server in order to function. After an undefined period of time, typically when revenue from a game or program declines, the publisher shuts down the server, preventing the user who paid for the program or game from using it. The digital product the customer paid for is rendered unusable by the publisher or simply taken away, and there is no law regulating it or defending the customer. In moments of writing this text, over 350 games were disabled/killed in the mentioned way, while almost 400 are at risk of this happening soon. The simple solution for the mentioned problem and practice of robbing customers of their digital products could be obligating the publishers, so that when their product reaches its end of support, they are obligated by law to release a software patch that disables the central server dependency of the program/game. Allowing the game/program to run in offline mode indefinitely. In that way, the customer could keep the program he paid for, while the publisher is not forced to keep their servers online. Making the whole situation "fair" again, as the Digital Fairness Act has in its name. Thank you kindly for your attention. Best regards, Krystian Kobus
